hard.next_step: June 17 Rick: Team leader looking into NDA, they have lost track. Rick offered to rerun NDA through our solution for quick turnaround.
hard.ais_why: Customer intrigued by Intake, iRisk, and iSupplier; direct interest in agentic/intake modules. Scoped as S2P plus ANA plus Intake. Qualified, $250K, created April 2026, NDA underway, discovery held.

north_star_verdict.headline: No two-way buyer contact in 67 days since the 30 April discovery; NDA stalled with buyer legal and Rick's 17 June expedite offer has drawn no reply, so the evaluation has not moved.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 30 April 2026; single discovery call 30 April; LastActivityDate 1 May. Stage advanced Qualified to Formal Evaluation on 29 June, but that was an internal admin move by manager Alexa Bradley, not buyer progression. Last genuine two-way buyer engagement was the 30 April call, 67 days ago (past the 60-day cold threshold). Rep-side NDA outreach on 17 June is awaiting a buyer reply. Close date 30 December is a placeholder 177 days out; buyer stated implementation is a next-year effort, so no near-term forcing event exists.
evidence: Single discovery call on 30 April 2026 with sourcing and IT (Edgar Karssenberg, Alex Boutrese, Suzanne, Raj Kumar, Vincent Jiao); no finance or executive budget owner present.

NDA is the current gate and it is stalled: Rick's Next Step on 17 June reports the buyer team leader 'looking into NDA, they have lost track', Rick offered to rerun it through Zycus for fast turnaround, and no buyer reply has followed.
No buyer-initiated contact since the 30 April discovery (67 days); zero OpportunityContactRoles logged; the thread runs single-threaded through Edgar and Suzanne, both silent.
Buyer set a deliberate, unhurried pace: Edgar said Simpson is 'investigating this calendar year' with 'implementation, training, go live... next year', with no compelling event or decision deadline.
Stage move to Formal Evaluation on 29 June was made internally by the owner's manager, not earned by a buyer milestone (no follow-up demo, no use cases returned, no pricing round).

metrics.narrative: Edgar Karssenberg framed the need qualitatively on the 30 April discovery call: Simpson is 'a 2,300,000,000 organization, publicly traded' that is 'big enough' for an eSourcing tool, with the two priorities being 'sourcing events' and spend visibility, wanting to 'get that to a place where we can just pull that more easily'. Alex Boutrese has 'done a great job getting a lot of the data' manually, which implies effort but was never quantified. No cost, cycle-time, headcount, savings target, or ROI figure was put on the table. The move is to co-build a quantified value model with Edgar anchored to current sourcing-event cycle time and manual spend-reporting hours, because without numbers there is no lever to pull finance in.
sources: Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Edgar Karssenberg: 'we're a 2,300,000,000 organization, publicly traded... our number one really is just sourcing events... also just with spend visibility'
Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Edgar Karssenberg on spend data: 'trying to get that to a place where we can just pull that more easily'

economic_buyer.narrative: No economic buyer is identified or engaged. On the 30 April call Suzanne described Edgar Karssenberg as 'our procurement lead in this investigation', and Edgar drives the sourcing agenda, but neither controls budget: Edgar himself flagged cost as a future question ('of course costing is going to be important... how it is priced'). No finance or CFO stakeholder attended, was named, or was offered as an introduction, and Kevin (Zycus) noted the ROI model is 'present to CFOs and their finance team' without any Simpson finance contact in the room. The evidence shows budget authority sits with an unengaged finance function; identifying and reaching that owner is a ranked move before any commercial step.
sources: Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Suzanne: 'he is our procurement lead in this investigation'
Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Edgar Karssenberg: 'I quickly start thinking about the cost... how it is priced'

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria surfaced clearly on the 30 April call even though no RFP or BRD yet exists. The buyer's stated must-haves are eSourcing event creation and management (Edgar's 'number one'), easier spend visibility and analytics (Edgar, Alex Boutrese asked how spend is classified, 'Is it by NAICS code?'), and Microsoft Teams-native workflow (Vincent Jiao probed Teams and Slack integration and a browser interface). Technical gating criteria came from IT: SAP integration and data residency/security (Raj Kumar: 'is it installed in our internal network, or is it cloud solution?... data security is more important') and the underlying AI/LLM (Vincent: 'what kind of the LLM... beneath the hood'). Invoicing is explicitly out of scope (Edgar) and iRisk is only a 'could have'. These are confirmed as interests but not yet weighted or formalized into a scored requirement document.
sources: Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Edgar Karssenberg: 'our number one really is just sourcing events'
Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Raj Kumar: 'is it installed in our internal network, or is it cloud solution?... data security is more important'
Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Vincent Jiao: on Teams/Slack integration and 'what kind of the LLM... beneath the hood'

decision_process.narrative: The buyer is at the very front of the process and said so: Suzanne stated 'we're certainly just starting down the path of starting to engage with vendors'. The agreed sequence out of the 30 April call was NDA first, then Simpson sends sourcing use cases, then Zycus returns with a solution consultant to run a tailored demo (Vincent: 'we would not send out the business use case until the NDA'). Timeline is buyer-controlled and slow: 'investigating this calendar year, implementation next year' (Edgar). No formal evaluation committee, scoring model, or approval chain has been described, and the process has not advanced past the NDA in 67 days. Reconfirming the evaluation steps and dates in writing is the way to make the process visible again.
sources: Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Suzanne: 'we're certainly just starting down the path... to engage with vendors'
Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Vincent Jiao: 'we would not send out the business use case until the NDA'
Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Edgar Karssenberg: 'investigating this calendar year... implementation next year'

paper_process.narrative: The only paper in motion is a mutual NDA and it is the choke point. On the 30 April call the buyer said the NDA 'should be initiated from our legal team' (Vincent Jiao) and Suzanne agreed to coordinate it. By 17 June the rep's Next Step reports the buyer team leader has 'lost track' of the NDA, and Rick offered to rerun it through Zycus's own e-signature for a quick turnaround, which has not been taken up. No MSA, order form, or SOW is relevant at this early stage. Visibility improves the moment the NDA is executed; the practical unlock is Rick sending a ready-to-sign NDA to a named legal owner rather than waiting on Simpson legal to restart.
sources: Salesforce Next_Step__c 17 June 2026: 'Team leader looking into NDA, they have lost track. Rick offered to rerun NDA through our solution for quick turnaround.'
Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Vincent Jiao: 'It should be initiated from our legal team'

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is real and owner-attributed, if unquantified. Simpson has no eSourcing tool and runs sourcing manually while relying on standard SAP plus custom BI reports for spend and PO tracking (Raj Kumar: 'we use the standard SAP... we develop the BI reports based on the user requirements'). Edgar Karssenberg owns the pain: the company was 'distracted by' an eight-year SAP go-live and has 'come to a point that we wanna tackle this', with spend data that is hard to pull easily. Confirmed pain is manual sourcing-event execution and fragmented spend visibility; the inferred pain is analyst time lost to manual data wrangling (implied by Alex Boutrese's manual data effort). The risk is that this pain is chronic and tolerated, not urgent.
sources: Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Edgar Karssenberg: 'we've had a lot of other things on our mind including SAP go live the last 8 years... now we've come to a point that we wanna tackle this'
Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Raj Kumar: 'we use the standard SAP... we develop the BI reports based on the user requirements'

champion.narrative: Edgar Karssenberg (Director, Strategic Sourcing) is the developing champion: he led the buyer introductions, named the priorities, asked commercially serious questions about pricing structure, and engaged warmly with the AI roadmap ('makes a lot of sense... sounds very interesting'). Suzanne is the coordinating sponsor on the technology side and accepted the point-of-contact and NDA role. What has developed is genuine sourcing-side interest and a named owner; what is missing is any advocacy behavior since 30 April, no introduction to a budget owner, no use cases returned, and 67 days of silence with the NDA drifting. This makes the champion weak and cooling rather than active, and rebuilding it is the precondition to any commercial progress.
sources: Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Edgar Karssenberg engagement on pricing and AI vision; Suzanne accepted NDA/point-of-contact role
Salesforce LastActivityDate 1 May 2026 and Next_Step__c 17 June 2026 show no buyer reply in 67 days

competition.narrative: There is no named vendor competitor in any source; the real competition is Do Nothing / Status Quo. Simpson today runs sourcing manually and uses standard SAP plus custom BI reports and an internal VIM/tracking setup for spend and PO visibility (Raj Kumar, 30 April), and its risk tooling sits in compliance partly on E2open, none of which is a sourcing rival. Edgar admitted an eight-year SAP go-live consumed attention and the buyer is only now 'investigating this calendar year' with no urgency. The threat is inertia: Simpson keeps managing sourcing manually and never signs. We win by converting the AI/agentic interest (Merlin intake, the Anna autonomous negotiation agent) into a quantified savings and cycle-time case that gives Edgar an internal reason to prioritize this over the status quo now.
sources: Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Raj Kumar: 'we have the SAP tools and BI... we use the standard SAP'
Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Edgar Karssenberg: 'investigating this calendar year, implementation next year'

deal_movement.summary: Internal record-keeping moved this run, not the buyer. Ownership was reassigned to Rick Taranek on 8 June, the stage was pushed Qualified to Formal Evaluation on 29 June by the owner's manager, and the division was recategorized to New Markets. The only buyer-facing event remains the 30 April discovery call; the 17 June NDA outreach is rep-side and unanswered.

items.change: Stage advanced Qualified to Formal Evaluation (internal move by manager Alexa Bradley, not a buyer milestone)
Division recategorized Zycus to New Markets
Rep NDA outreach: buyer legal 'lost track', Rick offered to expedite via Zycus e-signature; no buyer reply
Discovery call held with Simpson sourcing and IT; last two-way buyer engagement

competitive_position.summary: No vendor competitor has been named in any call, field, or activity; the single strongest current threat is Do Nothing / Status Quo. Simpson has no eSourcing tool and manages sourcing manually on standard SAP plus custom BI reports (Raj Kumar, 30 April 2026), and after an eight-year SAP go-live the buyer is exploring at a deliberate, no-deadline pace. The loss mode here is inertia and deprioritization, not a competitor: Simpson keeps running sourcing manually and never signs. There is no evidence of an RFP, RFI, or multi-vendor bake-off.

competitors.name: Do Nothing / Status Quo

competitors.source: Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026, Edgar Karssenberg; status-quo stack per Raj Kumar (standard SAP + custom BI reports)
competitors.how_we_win: Turn the Merlin intake and Anna autonomous-negotiation interest into a quantified sourcing-cycle and spend-visibility ROI, and attach a compelling event (a live sourcing event or FY spend target) so manual status quo becomes the costlier option.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Zycus fits the buyer's stated core needs well (sourcing events, spend analytics, Teams-native intake, agentic AI), with data-residency and SAP integration as the main areas still to be proven to IT.

best_practice_check.summary: The deal's whole risk is inertia, not a rival. The three highest-value levers are breaking the 67-day silence via the NDA, reaching a budget owner beyond sourcing, and manufacturing urgency from the AI value story.
flags: Break the 67-day silence now: owner sends Edgar and Suzanne a ready-to-sign NDA to restart the evaluation the buyer's own legal team dropped.
Multi-thread past a single sourcing contact: no budget owner is mapped, so identify and reach Simpson's finance/CFO sponsor before any pricing conversation.
Convert AI curiosity into urgency: put a quantified Merlin intake and Anna negotiation ROI in front of Edgar so this outranks living with manual SAP sourcing.

items.name: Edgar Karssenberg
items.title: Director, Strategic Sourcing
items.sentiment: Positive and engaged on 30 April: led introductions, named priorities, asked serious pricing questions, liked the AI roadmap. Silent 67 days since; developing but cooling.
items.risk: Single point of contact for the deal; if Edgar stays passive the evaluation goes dark. No evidence yet of executive access or internal advocacy.
items.source: Salesforce activity contact + Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026

items.name: Suzanne
items.title: Vice Director of Technology, North America Business Partner (self-stated on call)
items.sentiment: Supportive; agreed to coordinate the NDA and act as Zycus point of contact. Silent since 30 April and NDA has stalled under her coordination.
items.risk: Owns the stalled NDA coordination; if she does not re-engage legal, the evaluation stays blocked.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026 (self-introduction)

items.name: Alex Boutrese
items.title: Sourcing Analyst
items.sentiment: Engaged and curious; asked detailed spend-analytics and supplier-network questions. Evaluator, reports to Edgar; no decision authority.
items.risk: Low; technical evaluator, not a decision-maker.

items.risk: IT/security gate; unaddressed data-residency or integration concerns could slow or block later.

items.title: SAP lead / enterprise applications (self-stated on call)

items.risk: Guardian of the SAP status quo; his integration and security bar must be cleared for any technical fit.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026 (self-introduction)

champion_strength.summary: Edgar Karssenberg is a developing but weak and cooling champion. What developed on 30 April: he leads the sourcing investigation, named the priorities, asked commercial questions, and engaged with the AI roadmap. What is missing: any advocacy since, no introduction to a budget owner, no use cases returned, and 67 days of silence with the NDA drifting. Rebuilding Edgar (and securing an executive sponsor above him) is the precondition to any commercial step.
champion_strength.source: Avoma discovery call 30 April 2026; Salesforce LastActivityDate 1 May 2026; Next_Step__c 17 June 2026

ai_positioning_strength.summary: The agentic story was introduced well (intake orchestration in Teams, Merlin, and the Anna autonomous negotiation agent) and the buyer was intrigued, but it is not yet tied to any quantified value, so it reads as interesting rather than compelling. It is under-positioned until a numbers-backed AI use case lands with Edgar.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS is 6 / Moderate, and the calls corroborate it fairly: the buyer asked about the underlying LLM and was intrigued by intake and Anna, but showed curiosity rather than urgency and put invoicing/agentic touchless flows out of scope. AI Curious, not yet AI Hungry.

confidence_signals.summary: Engagement has cooled to cold: one discovery call, no buyer reply in 67 days, and a stalled NDA. The signal is inertia, not a lost bake-off.
items: No buyer-initiated contact since 30 April 2026 (67 days).
NDA gate stalled since 17 June with buyer legal having 'lost track' and no reply to Rick's expedite offer.
Buyer stated an unhurried, next-year implementation horizon with no compelling event.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: One promising discovery call with an interested sourcing lead (Edgar Karssenberg), but no budget owner, no urgency, and a 67-day silence with the NDA stalled: momentum and access, not product fit, are what stand between this and a real evaluation.

win_position.text: Zycus fits the buyer's core asks well: Edgar named sourcing events and spend visibility as priorities and Zycus leads on both, with Teams-native intake and agentic AI landing as intriguing on the 30 April call.
No economic buyer is engaged and cost was only flagged as a future question, so there is no one validated to fund a $250K purchase (Edgar Karssenberg, 30 April 2026).
The deal is single-threaded through sourcing and IT with no executive sponsor, and scope is already narrowing (invoicing out, iRisk a 'could-have'), a defensive early signal.

deal_momentum.text: No two-way buyer engagement in 67 days; the only recent activity is an internal stage push on 29 June by the owner's manager, not a buyer milestone.
The NDA gate has been stuck since 17 June with buyer legal having 'lost track' and no reply to Rick's offer to expedite it through Zycus.
Vincent Jiao asked Zycus to return with a solution-consultant demo built on Simpson's use cases, a genuine buyer pull to build on once the NDA clears (30 April 2026).

customer_commitment.text: The buyer set a deliberate 'investigating this calendar year, implementation next year' pace with no compelling event or decision deadline (Edgar Karssenberg, 30 April 2026).
Simpson still owes the sourcing use cases and a completed NDA, and has delivered neither in over two months, so no commitment beyond an exploratory first call exists.

deal_risk.text: The primary threat is inertia: Simpson keeps managing sourcing manually on SAP and custom BI and simply never prioritizes signing (Raj Kumar, 30 April 2026).
The close date of 30 December is a placeholder that contradicts the buyer's own next-year implementation horizon, so the forecast timing is not credible.

deal_momentum.evidence: 67 days with no buyer reply; NDA stalled; only internal admin stage move since discovery.

customer_commitment.evidence: No use cases returned, NDA incomplete, no urgency or compelling event; exploratory-only engagement.

deal_risk.evidence: Status-quo inertia plus scope narrowing (invoicing out, iRisk de-prioritized) and unvalidated budget.

scope_change.from: Source-to-Pay + ANA + Intake (full-suite framing per AIS scope)
scope_change.to: Source-to-Contract + Intake (sourcing events + spend visibility), with invoicing out of scope and iRisk a 'could-have'
scope_change.detail: On the 30 April call Edgar Karssenberg took invoicing off the table ('invoicing, it's out of scope for us') and rated iRisk a low-priority 'could have', narrowing the original S2P frame to S2C + Intake. This is a defensive scoping-down consistent with post-SAP-go-live caution and a phased rather than big-bang appetite.

customer_preference.evidence: Neutral and exploratory throughout the 30 April call ('investigating this calendar year'); polite interest in AI/Anna but no Zycus-specific lean, no commercial ask, and 67 days of silence since.

business_case.evidence: Qualitative pain only (manual sourcing, hard-to-pull spend data, post-SAP fatigue); no cost, savings, cycle-time, or ROI figure stated.
